CSE5313CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 20)

CSE5313 Coding and information theory for data science (Lecture 20)

Review for Private Information Retrieval

PIR from replicated databases

For 2 replicated databases, we have the following protocol:

  • User has i∼Umi \sim U_{m}i∼Um​.
  • User chooses r1,r2∼UF2mr_1, r_2 \sim U_{\mathbb{F}_2^m}r1​,r2​∼UF2m​​.
  • Two queries to each server:
    • q1,1=r1+eiq_{1, 1} = r_1 + e_iq1,1​=r1​+ei​, q1,2=r2q_{1, 2} = r_2q1,2​=r2​.
    • q2,1=r1q_{2, 1} = r_1q2,1​=r1​, q2,2=r2+eiq_{2, 2} = r_2 + e_iq2,2​=r2​+ei​.
  • Server jjj responds with qj,1cj⊤q_{j, 1} c_j^\topqj,1​cj⊤​ and qj,2cj⊤q_{j, 2} c_j^\topqj,2​cj⊤​.
  • Decoding?
    • q1,1c1⊤+q2,1c2⊤=r1c1+c2+eic1⊤=r1⋅0⊤+xi,1=xi,1q_{1, 1} c_1^\top + q_{2, 1} c_2^\top = r_1 c_1 + c_2 + e_i c_1^\top = r_1 \cdot 0^\top + x_{i, 1} = x_{i, 1}q1,1​c1⊤​+q2,1​c2⊤​=r1​c1​+c2​+ei​c1⊤​=r1​⋅0⊤+xi,1​=xi,1​.
    • q1,2c1⊤+q2,2c2⊤=r2c1+c2+eic2⊤=xi,2q_{1, 2} c_1^\top + q_{2, 2} c_2^\top = r_2 c_1 + c_2 + e_i c_2^\top = x_{i, 2}q1,2​c1⊤​+q2,2​c2⊤​=r2​c1​+c2​+ei​c2⊤​=xi,2​.

PIR-rate is k2k=12\frac{k}{2k} = \frac{1}{2}2kk​=21​.

PIR from coded parity-check databases

For 3 coded parity-check databases, we have the following protocol:

  • User has i∼Umi \sim U_{m}i∼Um​.
  • User chooses r1,r2,r3∼UF2mr_1, r_2, r_3 \sim U_{\mathbb{F}_2^m}r1​,r2​,r3​∼UF2m​​.
  • Three queries to each server:
    • q1,1=r1+eiq_{1, 1} = r_1 + e_iq1,1​=r1​+ei​, q1,2=r2q_{1, 2} = r_2q1,2​=r2​, q1,3=r3q_{1, 3} = r_3q1,3​=r3​.
    • q2,1=r1q_{2, 1} = r_1q2,1​=r1​, q2,2=r2+eiq_{2, 2} = r_2 + e_iq2,2​=r2​+ei​, q2,3=r3q_{2, 3} = r_3q2,3​=r3​.
    • q3,1=r1q_{3, 1} = r_1q3,1​=r1​, q3,2=r2q_{3, 2} = r_2q3,2​=r2​, q3,3=r3+eiq_{3, 3} = r_3 + e_iq3,3​=r3​+ei​.
  • Server jjj responds with qj,1cj⊤,qj,2cj⊤,qj,3cj⊤q_{j, 1} c_j^\top, q_{j, 2} c_j^\top, q_{j, 3} c_j^\topqj,1​cj⊤​,qj,2​cj⊤​,qj,3​cj⊤​.
  • Decoding?
    • q1,1c1⊤+q2,1c2⊤+q3,1c3⊤=r1c1+c2+c3+eic1⊤=r1⋅0⊤+xi,1=xi,1q_{1, 1} c_1^\top + q_{2, 1} c_2^\top + q_{3, 1} c_3^\top = r_1 c_1 + c_2 + c_3 + e_i c_1^\top = r_1 \cdot 0^\top + x_{i, 1} = x_{i, 1}q1,1​c1⊤​+q2,1​c2⊤​+q3,1​c3⊤​=r1​c1​+c2​+c3​+ei​c1⊤​=r1​⋅0⊤+xi,1​=xi,1​.
    • q1,2c1⊤+q2,2c2⊤+q3,2c3⊤=r2c1+c2+c3+eic2⊤=xi,2q_{1, 2} c_1^\top + q_{2, 2} c_2^\top + q_{3, 2} c_3^\top = r_2 c_1 + c_2 + c_3 + e_i c_2^\top = x_{i, 2}q1,2​c1⊤​+q2,2​c2⊤​+q3,2​c3⊤​=r2​c1​+c2​+c3​+ei​c2⊤​=xi,2​.
    • q1,3c1⊤+q2,3c2⊤+q3,3c3⊤=r3c1+c2+c3+eic3⊤=xi,3q_{1, 3} c_1^\top + q_{2, 3} c_2^\top + q_{3, 3} c_3^\top = r_3 c_1 + c_2 + c_3 + e_i c_3^\top = x_{i, 3}q1,3​c1⊤​+q2,3​c2⊤​+q3,3​c3⊤​=r3​c1​+c2​+c3​+ei​c3⊤​=xi,3​.

PIR-rate is k3k=13\frac{k}{3k} = \frac{1}{3}3kk​=31​.

Beyond z=1

Star-product theme

Given x=(x1,…,xj)j∈[n],y=(y1,…,yj)j∈[n]x=(x_1, \ldots, x_j)_{j\in [n]}, y=(y_1, \ldots, y_j)_{j\in [n]}x=(x1​,…,xj​)j∈[n]​,y=(y1​,…,yj​)j∈[n]​, over Fq\mathbb{F}_qFq​, the star-product is defined as:

x⋆y=(x1y1,…,xnyn)x \star y = (x_1 y_1, \ldots, x_n y_n)x⋆y=(x1​y1​,…,xn​yn​)

Given two linear codes, C,D⊆FqnC,D\subseteq \mathbb{F}_q^nC,D⊆Fqn​, the star-product code is defined as:

C⋆D=spanFq{x⋆y∣x∈C,y∈D}C \star D = span_{\mathbb{F}_q} \{x \star y | x \in C, y \in D\}C⋆D=spanFq​​{x⋆y∣x∈C,y∈D}

Singleton bound for star-product:

dC⋆D≤n−dim⁡C−dim⁡D+2d_{C \star D} \leq n-\dim C-\dim D+2dC⋆D​≤n−dimC−dimD+2

PIR form a database coded with any MDS code and z>1

To generalize the previous scheme to z>1z > 1z>1 need to encode multiple rrr‘s together.

  • As in the ramp scheme.

Recall from the ramp scheme, we use r1,…,rz∼UFqkr_1, \ldots, r_z \sim U_{\mathbb{F}_q^k}r1​,…,rz​∼UFqk​​ as our key vector to avoid occlusion of the servers.

In the star-product scheme:

  • Files are coded with an MDS code CCC.
  • The multiple rrr‘s are coded with an MDS code DDD.
  • The scheme is based on the minimum distance of C⋆DC \star DC⋆D.

To code the data:

  • Let C⊆FqnC \subseteq \mathbb{F}_q^nC⊆Fqn​ be an MDS code of dimension kkk.
  • For all j∈mj \in mj∈m, encode file xj=xj,1,…,xj,kx_j = x_{j, 1}, \ldots, x_{j, k}xj​=xj,1​,…,xj,k​ using GCG_CGC​:
(x1,1x1,2⋯x1,kx2,1x2,2⋯x2,k⋮⋮⋱⋮xm,1xm,2⋯xm,k)⋅GC=(c1,1c1,2⋯c1,nc2,1c2,2⋯c2,n⋮⋮⋱⋮cm,1cm,2⋯cm,n)\begin{pmatrix} x_{1, 1} & x_{1, 2} & \cdots & x_{1, k}\\ x_{2, 1} & x_{2, 2} & \cdots & x_{2, k}\\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\ x_{m, 1} & x_{m, 2} & \cdots & x_{m, k} \end{pmatrix} \cdot G_C = \begin{pmatrix} c_{1, 1} & c_{1, 2} & \cdots & c_{1, n}\\ c_{2, 1} & c_{2, 2} & \cdots & c_{2, n}\\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\ c_{m, 1} & c_{m, 2} & \cdots & c_{m, n} \end{pmatrix}​x1,1​x2,1​⋮xm,1​​x1,2​x2,2​⋮xm,2​​⋯⋯⋱⋯​x1,k​x2,k​⋮xm,k​​​⋅GC​=​c1,1​c2,1​⋮cm,1​​c1,2​c2,2​⋮cm,2​​⋯⋯⋱⋯​c1,n​c2,n​⋮cm,n​​​
  • For all j∈nj \in nj∈n, store cj=c1,j,c2,j,…,cm,jc_j = c_{1, j}, c_{2, j}, \ldots, c_{m, j}cj​=c1,j​,c2,j​,…,cm,j​ (a column of the above matrix) in server jjj.

Let r1,…,rz∼UFqkr_1, \ldots, r_z \sim U_{\mathbb{F}_q^k}r1​,…,rz​∼UFqk​​.

To code the queries:

  • Let D⊆FqkD \subseteq \mathbb{F}_q^kD⊆Fqk​ be an MDS code of dimension zzz.
  • Encode the rjr_jrj​‘s using GD=[g1⊤,…,gz⊤]G_D=[g_1^\top, \ldots, g_z^\top]GD​=[g1⊤​,…,gz⊤​].
(r1⊤,…,rz⊤)⋅GD=(r1,1r2,1⋯rz,1r1,2r2,2⋯rz,2⋮⋮⋱⋮r1,mr2,m⋯rz,m)⋅GD=((r1⊤,…,rz⊤)g1⊤,…,(r1⊤,…,rz⊤)gn⊤)(r_1^\top, \ldots, r_z^\top) \cdot G_D = \begin{pmatrix} r_{1, 1} & r_{2, 1} & \cdots & r_{z, 1}\\ r_{1, 2} & r_{2, 2} & \cdots & r_{z, 2}\\ \vdots & \vdots & \ddots & \vdots\\ r_{1, m} & r_{2, m} & \cdots & r_{z, m} \end{pmatrix} \cdot G_D=\left((r_1^\top,\ldots, r_z^\top)g_1^\top,\ldots, (r_1^\top,\ldots, r_z^\top)g_n^\top \right)(r1⊤​,…,rz⊤​)⋅GD​=​r1,1​r1,2​⋮r1,m​​r2,1​r2,2​⋮r2,m​​⋯⋯⋱⋯​rz,1​rz,2​⋮rz,m​​​⋅GD​=((r1⊤​,…,rz⊤​)g1⊤​,…,(r1⊤​,…,rz⊤​)gn⊤​)

To introduce the “errors in known locations” to the encoded rjr_jrj​‘s:

  • Let W∈{0,1}m×nW \in \{0, 1\}^{m \times n}W∈{0,1}m×n with some dC⋆D−1d_{C \star D} - 1dC⋆D​−1 entries in its iii-th row equal to 1.
  • These are the entries we will retrieve.

For every server j∈[n]j \in [n]j∈[n] send qj=r1⊤,…,rz⊤gj⊤+wjq_j = r_1^\top, \ldots, r_z^\top g_j^\top + w_jqj​=r1⊤​,…,rz⊤​gj⊤​+wj​, where wjw_jwj​ is the iii-th column of WWW.

  • This is similar to ramp scheme, where wjw_jwj​ is the “message”.
  • Privacy against collusion of zzz servers.

Response from server: aj=qjcj⊤a_j = q_j c_j^\topaj​=qj​cj⊤​.

Decoding? Let Q∈Fqm×nQ \in \mathbb{F}_q^{m \times n}Q∈Fqm×n​ be a matrix whose columns are the qjq_jqj​‘s.

Q=(r1⊤⋯rz⊤)⋅GD+WQ = \begin{pmatrix} r_1^\top & \cdots & r_z^\top \end{pmatrix} \cdot G_D + WQ=(r1⊤​​⋯​rz⊤​​)⋅GD​+W
  • The user has
\begin{aligned} q_1 c_1^\top, \ldots, q_n c_n^\top &= \left(\sum_{j \in m} q_{1, j} c_{j, 1}, \ldots, \sum_{j \in m} q_{n, j} c_{j, n}\right) \\ &=\sum_{j \in m} (q_{1,j}c_{j, 1}, \ldots, q_{n,j}c_{j, n}) \\ &=\sum_{j \in m} q^j \star c^j

where qjq^jqj is a row of QQQ and cjc^jcj is a codeword in CCC (an n,kn, kn,k qqq MDS code).

We have:

  • Q=(r1⊤,…,rz⊤)⋅GD+WQ=(r_1^\top, \ldots, r_z^\top) \cdot G_D + WQ=(r1⊤​,…,rz⊤​)⋅GD​+W
  • W∈{0,1}m×nW\in \{0, 1\}^{m \times n}W∈{0,1}m×n with some dC⋆D−1d_{C \star D} - 1dC⋆D​−1 entries in its iii-th row equal to 1.
  • (qj⋆cj)=sumj∈mqj⋆cj(q^j \star c^j)=sum_{j \in m} q^j \star c^j(qj⋆cj)=sumj∈m​qj⋆cj
  • Each qjq^jqj is a row of QQQ
    • For j≠ij \neq ij=i, qjq^jqj is a codeword in DDD
    • qi=di+wiq^i = d^i + w^iqi=di+wi
  • Therefore:
∑j∈[m]qj⋆cj=∑j≠i(dj⋆cj)+((di+wi)⋆ci)=∑j≠i(dj⋆cj)+wi⋆ci=(codeword in C⋆D)+(noise of Hamming weight ≤dC⋆D−1)\begin{aligned} \sum_{j \in [m]} q^j \star c^j &= \sum_{j \neq i} (d^j \star c^j) + ((d^i + w^i) \star c^i) \\ &= \sum_{j \neq i} (d^j \star c^j) + w^i \star c^i &= (\text{codeword in } C \star D )+( \text{noise of Hamming weight } \leq d_{C \star D} - 1) \end{aligned}j∈[m]∑​qj⋆cj​=j=i∑​(dj⋆cj)+((di+wi)⋆ci)=j=i∑​(dj⋆cj)+wi⋆ci​=(codeword in C⋆D)+(noise of Hamming weight ≤dC⋆D​−1)​

Multiply by HC⋆DH_{C \star D}HC⋆D​ and get dC⋆D−1d_{C \star D} - 1dC⋆D​−1 elements of cic^ici.

  • Recall that ci=xi⋅GCc^i = x_i \cdot G_Cci=xi​⋅GC​
  • Repeat kdC⋆D−1k^{d_{C \star D} - 1}kdC⋆D​−1 times to obtain kkk elements of cic^ici.
    • Suffices to obtain xix_ixi​, since CCC is n,kn, kn,k qqq MDS code.

PIR-rate:

  • = \frac{k}{# \text{ downloaded elements}} = \frac{k}{\frac{k}{d_{C \star D} - 1} \cdot n} = \frac{d_{C \star D} - 1}{n}
  • Singleton bound for star-product: dC⋆D≤n−dim⁡C−dim⁡D+2d_{C \star D} \leq n - \dim C - \dim D + 2dC⋆D​≤n−dimC−dimD+2.
  • Achieved with equality if CCC and DDD are Reed-Solomon codes.
  • PIR-rate = n−dim⁡C−dim⁡D+1n=n−k−z+1n\frac{n - \dim C - \dim D + 1}{n} = \frac{n - k - z + 1}{n}nn−dimC−dimD+1​=nn−k−z+1​.
  • Intuition:
    • “paying” kkk for “reconstruction from any kkk”.
    • “paying” zzz for “protection against colluding sets of size zzz”.
  • Capacity unknown! (as of 2022).
    • Known for special cases, e.g., k=1,z=1k = 1, z = 1k=1,z=1, certain types of schemes, etc.

PIR over graphs

Graph-based replication:

  • Every file is replicated twice on two separate servers.
  • Every two servers have at most one file in common.
  • “file” = “granularity” of data, i.e., the smallest information unit shared by any two servers.

A server that stores (xi,j)j=1d(x_{i, j})_{j=1}^d(xi,j​)j=1d​ receives (qi,j)j=1d(q_{i, j})_{j=1}^d(qi,j​)j=1d​, and replies with ∑j=1dqi,j⋅xi,j\sum_{j=1}^d q_{i, j} \cdot x_{i, j}∑j=1d​qi,j​⋅xi,j​.

The idea:

  • Consider a 2-server replicated PIR and “split” the queries between the servers.
  • Sum the responses, unwanted files “cancel out”, while xix_ixi​ does not.

Problem: Collusion.

Solution: Add per server randomness.

Good for any graph, and any q≥3q \geq 3q≥3 (for simplicity assume 2∣q2 | q2∣q).

The protocol:

  • Choose random γ∈Fqn\gamma \in \mathbb{F}_q^nγ∈Fqn​, ν∈Fqm\nu \in \mathbb{F}_q^mν∈Fqm​, and h∈F∖{0,1}h \in \mathbb{F} \setminus \{0, 1\}h∈F∖{0,1}.
  • Queries:
    • If node jjj is incident with edge ℓ\ellℓ, send qj,ℓ=γj⋅νℓq_{j, \ell} = \gamma_j \cdot \nu_\ellqj,ℓ​=γj​⋅νℓ​ to node jjj.
    • I.e., if server jjj stores file ℓ\ellℓ.
  • Except one node j0j_0j0​ that stores xix_ixi​, which gets qj0,i=h⋅γj0⋅νiq_{j_0, i} = h \cdot \gamma_{j_0} \cdot \nu_iqj0​,i​=h⋅γj0​​⋅νi​.
  • Server jjj responds with aj=∑j=1dqj,ℓ⋅xi,ℓa_j = \sum_{j=1}^d q_{j, \ell} \cdot x_{i, \ell}aj​=∑j=1d​qj,ℓ​⋅xi,ℓ​.
    • Where xi,1,…,x_{i, 1}, \ldots, xi,1​,…,x_{i, d}$ are the files adjacent with it.

Example

  • Consider the following graph.
  • n=5,m=7,andi=3n = 5, m = 7, and i = 3n=5,m=7,andi=3.
  • q3=γ3⋅v2,v3,v6q_3 = \gamma_3 \cdot v_2, v_3, v_6q3​=γ3​⋅v2​,v3​,v6​ and a3=x2⋅γ3v2+x3⋅γ3v3+x6⋅γ3v6a_3 = x_2 \cdot \gamma_3 v_2 + x_3 \cdot \gamma_3 v_3 + x_6 \cdot \gamma_3 v_6a3​=x2​⋅γ3​v2​+x3​⋅γ3​v3​+x6​⋅γ3​v6​.
  • q2=γ2⋅v1,hv3,v4q_2 = \gamma_2 \cdot v_1, h v_3, v_4q2​=γ2​⋅v1​,hv3​,v4​ and a2=x1⋅γ2v1+x3⋅hγ2v3+x4⋅γ2v4a_2 = x_1 \cdot \gamma_2 v_1 + x_3 \cdot h \gamma_2 v_3 + x_4 \cdot \gamma_2 v_4a2​=x1​⋅γ2​v1​+x3​⋅hγ2​v3​+x4​⋅γ2​v4​.

Correctness:

  • ∑j=15γj−1aj=(h+1)v3x3\sum_{j=1}^5 \gamma_j^{-1} a_j =( h + 1 )v_3 x_3∑j=15​γj−1​aj​=(h+1)v3​x3​
  • h≠1,v3≠0  ⟹  h \neq 1, v_3 \neq 0 \impliesh=1,v3​=0⟹ find x3x_3x3​.

Parameters:

  • Storage overhead 2 (for any graph).
  • Download n⋅kn \cdot kn⋅k.
  • PIR rate 1/n.

Collusion resistance:

1-privacy: Each node sees an entirely random vector.

2-privacy:

  • If no edge – as for 1-privacy.
  • If edge exists – E.g.,
    • γ3v6\gamma_3 v_6γ3​v6​ and γ4v6\gamma_4 v_6γ4​v6​ are independent.
    • γ3v3\gamma_3 v_3γ3​v3​ and h⋅γ2v3h \cdot \gamma_2 v_3h⋅γ2​v3​ are independent.

S-privacy:

  • Let S⊆nS \subseteq nS⊆n (e.g., S=2,3,5S = 2,3,5S=2,3,5), and consider the query matrix of their mutual files:
QS=diag(γ3,γ2,γ5)(1h11)diag(v3,v4)Q_S = diag(\gamma_3, \gamma_2, \gamma_5) \begin{pmatrix} 1 &\\ h & 1 \\ & 1\end{pmatrix} diag(v_3, v_4)QS​=diag(γ3​,γ2​,γ5​)​1h​11​​diag(v3​,v4​)
  • It can be shown that Pr(QS)=1(q−1)4Pr(Q_S)=\frac{1}{(q-1)^4}Pr(QS​)=(q−1)41​, regardless of i  ⟹  i \impliesi⟹ perfect privacy.
